description: Origin of the gate assignment decision. CDM: Collaborative Decision Making system; Manual: dispatcher override; Auto-assign: automated allocation; Rescheduled: changed from original; IROP: irregular operations reassignment.
Timestamp when the gate was reserved for this flight, typically earlier than scheduled gate-in to allow for ground equipment setup and early arrival buffer.
reference: SITA Airport Management Platform - Gate Assignment Module

description: Current lifecycle status of the gate assignment. Planned: initial assignment; Confirmed: verified by operations; Active: aircraft at gate; Completed: turnaround finished; Released: gate available; Cancelled: assignment voided.
reference: SITA Airport Management Platform - Gate Assignment Module

description: Timestamp when this gate assignment record was initially created in the system. Represents the business event time of assignment decision, distinct from technical audit timestamps.
reference: SITA Airport Management Platform - Gate Assignment Module

description: Current CDM milestone achieved for this gate assignment. TOBT: Target Off-Block Time set; TSAT: Target Start-Up Approval Time issued; AOBT: Actual Off-Block Time; AIBT: Actual In-Block Time; ALDT: Actual Landing Time.
Indicates whether this gate assignment has a scheduling conflict with another assignment (overlapping time windows). True when conflict detected; requires resolution by operations.
reference: SITA Airport Management Platform - Gate Assignment Module

description: Free-text notes documenting how a gate assignment conflict was resolved, including dispatcher actions, alternative gate assignments, or operational adjustments made.
reference: SITA Airport Management Platform - Gate Assignment Module

description: The gate number or stand identifier at the origin airport from which the flight departed. [Moved from flight_leg: departure_gate is a STRING attribute in flight_leg that represents the gate assignment at departure. This should be replaced by the gate_assignment association, as a flight can be reassigned to multiple gates during IROP. The actual gate used is determined by querying gate_assignment with assignment_type='departure' or 'turnaround' and assignment_status='completed'.]

Indicates whether the flight assigned to this gate is domestic or international. Schengen applies to intra-European flights with no border control. Determines gate area assignment and customs/immigration requirements.
Free-text explanation for any gate hold or delay in releasing the gate assignment. Documents operational reasons such as late arriving aircraft, maintenance issues, or ATC delays.

Classification of gate facility type. Contact: jet bridge equipped; Remote: bus boarding; Hardstand: tarmac parking; Cargo: freight operations. Determines boarding method and ground service requirements.
Indicates whether ground power unit (GPU) is available at this gate for this assignment. Required for aircraft electrical power during turnaround when APU is shut down.
reference: IATA Airport Handling Manual (AHM) - Ground Support Equipment

description: Classification of airport operational role in airline network. Hub: major connecting airport; Spoke: destination from hub; Focus city: secondary hub; Outstation: remote station with limited operations.
Indicates whether a jet bridge (passenger boarding bridge) is available and operational at this gate for this assignment. Critical for passenger boarding operations and accessibility requirements.
Timestamp when this gate assignment record was last updated. Tracks changes to assignment details, status transitions, or operational adjustments.
reference: SITA Airport Management Platform - Gate Assignment Module

description: Indicates whether Passenger Facility Charge applies to this gate assignment based on airport authority regulations. Used for financial settlement and regulatory reporting to DOT.
reference: US DOT 14 CFR Part 158 - Passenger Facility Charges

description: Indicates whether preconditioned air (PCA) system is available at this gate for cabin climate control during turnaround. Reduces APU usage and fuel burn.
reference: IATA Airport Handling Manual (AHM) - Ground Support Equipment

description: Indicates whether this assignment received priority treatment overriding normal allocation rules. True for VIP flights, IROP recovery, or operational priority situations.
reference: SITA Airport Management Platform - Gate Assignment Module

description: Business reason for priority override of normal gate assignment rules. Documents justification for preferential gate allocation outside standard procedures. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: vip_flight|irop_recovery|maintenance_priority|diplomatic|medical_emergency|operational_necessity|none — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Free-text operational notes and comments related to this gate assignment. Used by dispatchers and ground operations for coordination and situational awareness.
reference: SITA Airport Management Platform - Gate Assignment Module

description: Planned timestamp when aircraft is scheduled to arrive at the gate and set parking brake (On-block time from OOOI). Used for gate allocation planning and OTP reporting.
Planned timestamp when aircraft is scheduled to push back from gate and release parking brake (Off-block time from OOOI). Used for gate release planning and next assignment scheduling.
Indicates whether this gate assignment requires coordination with airport slot allocation due to capacity constraints at slot-controlled airport. True for Level 3 coordinated airports.

Special Service Request code indicating special handling requirements for this gate assignment (e.g., WCHR for wheelchair, DEPA for deportee, MEDA for medical). Drives ground service and security coordination.
reference: IATA Passenger Services Conference Resolutions Manual (PSCRM)

description: ICAO aerodrome reference code indicating maximum aircraft size the gate can accommodate (A through F). Ensures aircraft-gate physical compatibility for safe operations.
Planned duration in minutes for aircraft turnaround at the gate, from gate-in to gate-out. Varies by aircraft type, service level, and operational requirements.
reference: IATA Airport Handling Manual (AHM) - Turnaround Standards

description: Unique identifier for the airport terminal facility record. Primary key.

Indicates whether the terminal facility meets accessibility standards for passengers with reduced mobility (PRM) including wheelchair access, accessible counters, and assistance services.

Postal or ZIP code for the terminal facility address.
Classification of the terminal facility structure type.
Strategic classification of the airport station in the airline's network (hub = central connecting airport, spoke = route from hub to destination).
Date of the most recent major renovation or modernization project completed for this terminal facility.
Number of airline lounges operated within this terminal facility for premium passengers and Frequent Flyer Program (FFP) members.

IATA airline code of the primary operating carrier for this terminal facility (null for joint-use or airport authority facilities).
Current operational state of the terminal facility.
Designation of facility ownership and usage rights (airline-owned, joint-use with other carriers, or airport authority managed).
Design capacity of the terminal facility measured in passengers (PAX) that can be processed per hour during peak operations.

Timestamp when this terminal facility record was last modified in the system.
Number of remote gates requiring bus transportation for passenger boarding (no jet bridge connection).
Number of Transportation Security Administration (TSA) or equivalent security screening checkpoints in the terminal facility.
Number of self-service check-in kiosks (CUSS - Common Use Self-Service) available for passenger self-check-in.
Indicates whether the airport requires slot coordination (allocated takeoff and landing times) due to capacity constraints.
Total floor area of the terminal facility measured in square meters.
Business identifier for the terminal within the airport (e.g., Terminal 1, Terminal A, Concourse B, Satellite Terminal).
Total number of aircraft gates available in this terminal facility for passenger boarding and deplaning operations.
Unique identifier for the crew absence record. Primary key.
Identifier of the crew member who is absent. Links to the crew member master record.
Identifier of the crew member assigned to replace the absent crew member. Nullable if no replacement has been assigned yet.
FK to crew.crew_base

Foreign key linking to finance.general_ledger. Business justification: Crew absences trigger sick pay, statutory leave payments, and replacement crew costs posted to GL. Airline HR-finance integration requires absence→GL traceability for sick pay accruals, insurance reco

Foreign key linking to crew.medical_certificate. Business justification: Normalize crew_absence to reference medical_certificate by FK. Currently crew_absence stores medical_certificate_reference (STRING), medical_certificate_issued_date, and medical_certificate_expiry_dat

Foreign key linking to safety.occurrence. Business justification: Crew absences due to safety events (injuries, fatigue, medical issues from incidents) must be linked to the safety occurrence for SMS tracking and trend analysis. This FK enables correlation of crew a
Identifier of the previous absence record if this absence is a recurrence or continuation. Nullable if this is the first occurrence.

Foreign key linking to airport.station. Business justification: Absences are reported and managed at crew's assigned station for operational impact assessment, replacement crew sourcing, and station-level crew availability tracking. Critical for daily operations c
Current lifecycle status of the absence record: pending approval, approved, rejected, active (ongoing), completed, or cancelled.

Classification of the absence reason: sick leave, maternity/paternity leave, compassionate leave, suspension, administrative leave, or unplanned absence. description: The number of scheduled duty periods impacted by this absence, requiring roster adjustments or crew reassignments.
The number of individual flight legs that the absent crew member was originally scheduled to operate, now requiring coverage.

The name or identifier of the medical examiner, HR officer, or other authority who issued the return-to-duty clearance.

description: Reference to the specific clause or section of the collective bargaining agreement that governs this type of absence (e.g., sick leave policy, maternity leave entitlement).

description: The date and time when this absence record was first created in the system.
The operational position or role of the absent crew member: captain, first officer, flight engineer, purser, flight attendant, or cabin crew.

description: Total number of calendar days the crew member is absent, calculated from start date to end date inclusive.
The date when the crew absence period ends. Nullable for open-ended absences.
The date and time when this absence record was last updated or modified.
The channel through which the absence was reported: phone, email, mobile app, in-person, crew portal, or third-party notification.
The date and time when the crew member or representative notified the airline of the absence.
Indicates whether the crew member receives full pay, no pay, or partial pay during the absence period, as per company policy and collective agreement.

description: Free-text notes providing additional context or details about the reason for the absence. May contain sensitive personal information.
Indicates whether this absence is a recurrence of a previous absence for the same or related reason. True if recurrent, False otherwise. Used for absence pattern monitoring.
Indicates whether a replacement crew member is required to cover the absent crew member's scheduled duties. True if replacement is needed, False otherwise.
Indicates whether the crew member has received medical or administrative clearance to return to duty. True if cleared, False otherwise.
reference: FAA Medical Certification Standards, EASA Medical Requirements

description: The actual date when the crew member returned to active duty following the absence. Nullable if the crew member has not yet returned.
The date when the crew absence period begins.

business_glossary_term: Flight Time Limitations (FTL) Legality Check Identifier
description: Unique identifier for the FTL legality check record. Primary key.
Identifier of the duty period being evaluated for legality. Nullable if check is pairing-level.
Identifier of the crew member being assessed for fitness to operate.

Foreign key linking to safety.occurrence. Business justification: FTL violations are a primary causal factor in fatigue-related safety occurrences. EASA ORO.FTL and FAR Part 117 require linking FTL breaches to safety events. ftl_legality_check has no occurrence link
Identifier of the crew pairing being evaluated. Nullable if check is duty-period-level.
Date and time when the check result, waiver, or mitigation was approved by the authority. Nullable for automated pass results.
Name or identifier of the authority who approved the check result, waiver, or mitigation (e.g., Crew Control Manager, Chief Pilot, Duty Manager). Nullable for automated pass results.
Method used for the fitness assessment: regulatory_rule (FAR Part 117, EASA FTL rule-based check) or biomathematical_model (SAFE, FAID, FRMS model-based fatigue risk assessment).
reference: FAR Part 117, EASA FTL, ICAO Doc 9966

description: Free-text notes or comments regarding the legality check, including rationale for waivers, mitigation details, or special circumstances. Nullable if no additional context required.
Date and time when the FTL legality or fatigue risk check was executed.

Event or condition that triggered the legality check: pre-assignment (during rostering), pre-departure (gate check), post-duty (compliance audit), IROP extension (irregular operations duty extension request), manual audit, or scheduled review.
reference: FAR Part 117, EASA FTL, ICAO Doc 9966

description: Categorical fatigue risk band derived from the fatigue score: low, moderate, elevated, high, or critical. Nullable for regulatory rule-based checks.
Numeric fatigue risk score generated by the biomathematical model (e.g., SAFE effectiveness score, FAID fatigue index). Nullable for regulatory rule-based checks.
reference: ICAO Doc 9966 (FRMS), SAFE Model, FAID Quantum

description: Actual or projected FDP duration in hours for the duty period being evaluated.
Maximum allowable FDP duration in hours per the applicable regulation, adjusted for number of sectors, time of day, and crew augmentation.

Overall fitness-to-operate result: legal (crew is legal and fit), illegal (crew exceeds FTL limits), waiver_granted (regulatory waiver issued for non-compliance), or elevated_risk_accepted (biomathematical model shows elevated risk but accepted with mitigation).
reference: FAR Part 117, EASA FTL, ICAO Doc 9966 (FRMS)

description: Cumulative flight time in hours for the crew member over the preceding 28-day rolling window at the time of check.
Cumulative flight time in hours for the crew member over the preceding 365-day rolling window at the time of check.

Description of any mitigation measures applied to address elevated fatigue risk or legality concerns (e.g., crew augmentation, duty period reduction, additional rest, flight reassignment). Nullable if no mitigation required.
Date and time when this legality check record was first created in the system.
Date and time when this legality check record was last updated in the system.
Status of rest period compliance: compliant (meets minimum), non_compliant (below minimum), reduced_rest_approved (approved reduced rest per regulation), or waiver_granted (regulatory waiver issued).
Actual rest period in hours provided or projected before the next duty period.
Minimum rest period required in hours before the next duty period per applicable regulation.
Source system or module that generated the legality check record (e.g., AIMS Crew Management System, SAFE Model Engine, FAID Quantum, Manual Entry).

description: Unique identifier for the crew aviation licence record. Primary key.

Reference to the crew member who holds this licence.
The timestamp when this licence record was first created in the system.
URL or file path to the scanned or electronic copy of the licence document stored in the document management system.
The date on which the licence expires and is no longer valid unless renewed. Null if the licence has no expiration (lifetime validity).
The date on which the licence was originally issued by the regulatory authority.
The date on which the language proficiency endorsement expires and must be re-assessed. Null if no expiry (level 6 Expert has no expiry).
ICAO language proficiency rating for aviation English (or other endorsed language). Scale 1-6, where 4 (Operational) is the minimum for international operations, 6 is Expert. Per ICAO Annex 1.
The unique licence number issued by the regulatory authority. This is the official identifier on the physical or electronic licence document.

Current lifecycle status of the licence: valid (active and in good standing), expired (past expiry date), suspended (temporarily invalid), revoked (permanently cancelled), pending_renewal (renewal in process), surrendered (voluntarily returned).
The category of aviation licence. ATPL (Airline Transport Pilot Licence), CPL (Commercial Pilot Licence), MPL (Multi-crew Pilot Licence), cabin crew attestation, flight engineer, or flight navigator.
Free-text field for additional notes, comments, or special instructions related to this licence record.

Comma-separated list of ratings and endorsements held on this licence (e.g., instrument rating, multi-engine, type ratings). This is a summary field; detailed type ratings are tracked in the qualification product.
The date on which the licence was last renewed. Null if the licence has never been renewed since original issue.
Free-text description of any restrictions or limitations placed on the licence by the issuing authority (e.g., 'Valid only with corrective lenses', 'VFR only', 'Co-pilot only').
The date on which the licence was permanently revoked by the issuing authority. Null if the licence has never been revoked.
Free-text description of the reason for licence revocation (e.g., serious safety violation, fraud, criminal conviction).
The date on which a suspension of the licence is scheduled to end or was lifted. Null if currently suspended with no defined end date.
Free-text description of the reason for licence suspension (e.g., medical disqualification, regulatory violation, administrative hold).
The date on which a suspension of the licence became effective. Null if the licence has never been suspended.
The total flight hours logged by the crew member at the time the licence was issued. Captured for historical record and regulatory audit.

business_glossary_term: Medical Certificate Accounts Payable Id (Foreign Key)

Identifier of the crew member to whom this medical certificate belongs. Links to crew member master record.
ICAO medical certificate class. Class 1 for airline transport pilots and commercial pilots, Class 2 for private pilots and cabin crew, Class 3 for air traffic controllers.
reference: ICAO Annex 1, FAA Part 67, EASA Part-MED

description: Unique certificate number issued by the Aviation Medical Examiner (AME) or Designated Aviation Medical Examiner (DAME). Official identifier for regulatory tracking.
Type of medical certificate issuance. Initial for first-time, Renewal for standard periodic renewal, Revalidation for extension, Special Issuance for conditional approval with limitations or waivers.
Timestamp when this medical certificate record was first created in the AIMS Crew Management System.
URL or file path reference to the scanned or digital copy of the physical medical certificate document stored in the document management system.
Date the crew member underwent the medical examination that resulted in this certificate issuance. May differ from issue date if additional review or approval was required.

Type of medical examination conducted. Standard for routine periodic examinations, Extended for comprehensive examinations with additional tests, Special for examinations required due to medical events or regulatory requests.
Date the medical certificate expires. Crew member cannot operate beyond this date without a valid renewed certificate. Critical for crew legality checks.
reference: FAA Part 67, EASA Part-MED, ICAO Annex 1

description: Indicates whether the crew member requires a hearing aid to meet hearing standards. True if hearing aid is required, False otherwise.

License or designation number of the Aviation Medical Examiner (AME) or Designated Aviation Medical Examiner (DAME) who issued the certificate. Used for regulatory verification.
Name of the Aviation Medical Examiner (AME) or Designated Aviation Medical Examiner (DAME) who issued the certificate.
ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country code of the country where the medical certificate was issued. Determines applicable regulatory authority (FAA, EASA, etc.).

Any operational limitations or restrictions imposed on the crew member as a condition of the medical certificate. Examples include corrective lenses required, valid only with co-pilot, daytime operations only, or specific medical monitoring requirements.
Date by which the crew member must undergo the next medical examination to maintain continuous medical certification. Used for proactive crew scheduling and compliance planning.

Additional remarks, notes, or comments from the Aviation Medical Examiner (AME) or regulatory authority regarding the medical certificate. May include follow-up requirements, monitoring instructions, or clarifications.

Indicates whether this certificate was issued under special issuance provisions due to a medical condition that does not meet standard requirements but has been granted a waiver or authorization. True if special issuance, False for standard issuance.
Timestamp when this medical certificate record was last updated in the AIMS Crew Management System. Tracks any modifications to certificate details, status changes, or verification updates.

Current validity status of the medical certificate. Valid indicates the certificate is active and within its validity period. Expired indicates the certificate has passed its expiry date. Suspended or Revoked indicates regulatory action. Pending indicates awaiting final approval or special issuance decision.
Date the medical certificate was last verified with the issuing regulatory authority or through the AIMS Crew Management System.

Status of verification of the medical certificate with the issuing regulatory authority. Verified indicates the certificate has been confirmed as authentic and valid with the authority. Used for compliance audits and crew legality validation.
Indicates whether the crew member requires corrective lenses (glasses or contact lenses) to meet vision standards. True if corrective lenses are required, False otherwise.
Any medical waivers or special issuance conditions granted by the regulatory authority. Indicates exceptions to standard medical requirements with documented justification.

Foreign key linking to airport.station. Business justification: FTL compliance reporting and crew positioning require duty periods to be formally anchored to their start station. Aviation regulators and crew control systems track where each duty period originates.

business_glossary_term: Duty Period General Ledger Id (Foreign Key)

Reference to the crew member assigned to this duty period.

Foreign key linking to safety.occurrence. Business justification: Occurrences happen during specific duty periods. Essential for fatigue analysis, FTL investigation, and causal factor determination. Real business process: occurrence context analysis and fatigue risk
Reference to the pairing that contains this duty period. A pairing is a sequence of duty periods and rest periods assigned to a crew member.

Indicates whether the crew member is acclimated to the local time zone at duty start. Acclimated means the crew member has been in the time zone for sufficient time per FAR Part 117 definitions. Affects maximum FDP limits.
Indicates whether this duty period was actually operated (True) or was planned but not executed (False). Used for payroll and compliance reporting.
Indicates whether this duty period involves augmented crew (additional crew members allowing in-flight rest). True if augmented, False otherwise. Affects maximum FDP limits.
Total block time (gate departure to gate arrival) in minutes for all flight segments within this duty period. Null for non-flight duty types.
Code indicating why the duty period was cancelled if actual_flag is False (e.g., CREW_SICK, AIRCRAFT_MAINTENANCE, WEATHER, SCHEDULE_CHANGE). Null if duty was operated.
Timestamp when this duty period record was first created in the system.
Three-letter IATA airport code where the duty period ends (release location).
Sequential number of this duty period within the pairing (1, 2, 3, etc.).

Classification of the duty period: flight duty (operating flights), ground duty (administrative/ground tasks), deadhead (crew repositioning without revenue duty), training (simulator/classroom), standby (on-call at airport), or reserve (on-call at home/hotel).

business_glossary_term: European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) Flight Time Limitations (FTL) Compliant Flag
description: Indicates whether this duty period is compliant with EASA FTL regulations. True if compliant, False if violation detected.

business_glossary_term: Federal Aviation Regulation (FAR) Part 117 Compliant Flag
description: Indicates whether this duty period is compliant with FAA FAR Part 117 flight and duty time limitations. True if compliant, False if violation detected.
Categorical fatigue risk level derived from the fatigue risk score: low (acceptable), moderate (monitor), high (mitigate), critical (unacceptable).

Calculated fatigue risk score for this duty period based on biomathematical fatigue models (e.g., SAFTE-FAST, Boeing Alertness Model). Higher scores indicate higher fatigue risk. Scale typically 0-100.

business_glossary_term: Flight Duty Period (FDP) Elapsed Time in Minutes
description: Total elapsed time in minutes from FDP start to FDP end. Critical for FAR Part 117 and EASA FTL maximum FDP limit checks. Null for non-flight duty types.
Timestamp marking the end of the flight duty period, which ends when the aircraft finally comes to rest and the engines are shut down at the last flight segment. Null for non-flight duty types.
Timestamp marking the start of the flight duty period, which begins when a crew member is required to report for duty with the intention of conducting a flight. Null for non-flight duty types.
Number of flight segments (takeoff and landing cycles) operated during this duty period. Used for fatigue risk assessment. Null for non-flight duty types.
Timestamp when this duty period record was last modified in the system.

Overall legality status of the duty period considering all applicable regulations (FAR Part 117, EASA FTL, company policies). Legal means all checks passed; illegal means one or more violations detected; waiver_granted means a regulatory waiver was approved; under_review means legality is being assessed.
Comma-separated list of violation codes if the duty period is illegal (e.g., MAX_FDP_EXCEEDED, INSUFFICIENT_REST, MAX_DUTY_EXCEEDED). Null if legal.
Timestamp when the crew member is released from duty. Marks the end of the duty period.
Timestamp when the crew member is required to report for duty. Marks the start of the duty period.

business_glossary_term: Rest Period After Duty in Minutes
description: Duration in minutes of the rest period immediately following this duty period. Used to verify minimum rest requirements per FAR Part 117 and EASA FTL.

business_glossary_term: Rest Period Before Duty in Minutes
description: Duration in minutes of the rest period immediately preceding this duty period. Used to verify minimum rest requirements per FAR Part 117 and EASA FTL.
Indicates whether this duty period was part of the original published schedule (True) or was added/modified after publication (False). Used for operational performance tracking.
Name of the operational system that originated this duty period record (e.g., AIMS, NetLine/Ops).
Indicates whether this duty period qualifies as split duty (duty period interrupted by a rest opportunity). True if split duty, False otherwise. Affects FDP extension calculations.

business_glossary_term: Split Duty Rest Period in Minutes
description: Duration in minutes of the rest opportunity during a split duty period. Null if not a split duty. Used to calculate FDP extensions.
Number of time zones crossed during this duty period. Used for fatigue risk and circadian disruption assessment.
IANA time zone identifier for the duty end station (e.g., Europe/London). Used for circadian rhythm and fatigue risk calculations.
IANA time zone identifier for the duty start station (e.g., America/New_York). Used for circadian rhythm and fatigue risk calculations.

business_glossary_term: Total Duty Elapsed Time (TDT) in Minutes
description: Total elapsed time in minutes from report time to release time. Used for FAR Part 117 and EASA FTL compliance calculations.

description: Reference to the audit or inspection report that validated compliance with ETOPS requirements prior to authorization issuance. May include document ID or inspection case number.

business_glossary_term: ETOPS (Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards) Authorization Status
description: Current lifecycle status of the ETOPS authorization. Active authorizations are valid for dispatch; suspended, expired, or revoked authorizations prohibit ETOPS operations.
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA AC 120-42B, EASA Part-CAT

business_glossary_term: Cargo Fire Suppression ETOPS (Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards) Compliance Flag
description: Indicates whether the cargo compartment fire suppression system meets ETOPS requirements for extended diversion times. True if compliant; false otherwise.
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA 14 CFR 121.161, EASA Part-CAT

business_glossary_term: Communication System ETOPS (Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards) Compliance Flag
description: Indicates whether the aircraft communication systems (HF, SATCOM, ACARS) meet ETOPS requirements for extended overwater operations. True if compliant; false otherwise.
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA AC 120-42B, EASA Part-CAT

description: The timestamp when this ETOPS authorization record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail and data lineage.

business_glossary_term: ETOPS (Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards) Approved Diversion Time in Minutes
description: The maximum approved diversion time in minutes that the aircraft is authorized to operate from the nearest suitable alternate airport. Common values include 120, 138, 180, 207, or 240 minutes.
reference: ICAO Annex 6 Part I, FAA 14 CFR 121.161, EASA Part-CAT

business_glossary_term: ETOPS (Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards) Authorization Effective Date
description: The date on which this ETOPS authorization becomes valid and the aircraft is approved to commence ETOPS operations under this authorization.
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA 14 CFR 121.161, EASA Part-CAT

business_glossary_term: Electrical System ETOPS (Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards) Compliance Flag
description: Indicates whether the aircraft electrical power generation and distribution systems meet ETOPS redundancy standards. True if compliant; false otherwise.
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA AC 120-42B, EASA Part-CAT

description: ICAO or manufacturer engine type designator for the engines installed on this aircraft under this ETOPS authorization (e.g., CFM56-7B, GE90-115B). ETOPS authorization is engine-type specific.
reference: ICAO Engine Type Designators, FAA Type Certificate Data Sheet

business_glossary_term: ETOPS (Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards) Authorization Expiry Date
description: The date on which this ETOPS authorization expires and is no longer valid for dispatch. Nullable for authorizations without a fixed expiry (subject to continuous compliance).
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA AC 120-42B, EASA Part-CAT

business_glossary_term: Fuel System ETOPS (Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards) Compliance Flag
description: Indicates whether the aircraft fuel system (including pumps, crossfeed, and monitoring) meets ETOPS reliability and redundancy standards. True if compliant; false otherwise.
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA AC 120-42B, EASA Part-CAT

business_glossary_term: Hydraulic System ETOPS (Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards) Compliance Flag
description: Indicates whether the aircraft hydraulic systems meet ETOPS redundancy and reliability requirements. True if compliant; false otherwise.
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA AC 120-42B, EASA Part-CAT

description: The date of the most recent compliance verification or audit confirming that the aircraft and operator continue to meet ETOPS standards. Used to track ongoing compliance.
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA AC 120-42B, EASA Part-CAT, Internal Compliance Standard

description: The timestamp when this ETOPS authorization record was last updated. Tracks the most recent change to any field in the record.

business_glossary_term: ETOPS (Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards) Maintenance Program Reference
description: Reference to the approved ETOPS maintenance program document or section number that this authorization is contingent upon. Compliance with this program is mandatory for maintaining authorization validity.
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA AC 120-42B, EASA Part-CAT, Operator Maintenance Program

business_glossary_term: MEL (Minimum Equipment List) Reference for ETOPS (Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards)
description: Reference to the specific MEL section or revision that governs ETOPS dispatch requirements for this aircraft. Identifies which equipment must be operational for ETOPS flight.
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA AC 120-42B, EASA Part-CAT, Operator MEL

business_glossary_term: Navigation System ETOPS (Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards) Compliance Flag
description: Indicates whether the aircraft navigation systems (FMS, GPS, INS) meet ETOPS accuracy and redundancy requirements. True if compliant; false otherwise.
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA AC 120-42B, EASA Part-CAT

description: The scheduled date for the next compliance review or audit to ensure continued ETOPS authorization validity. Nullable if no periodic review is mandated.
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA AC 120-42B, EASA Part-CAT, Internal Compliance Standard

business_glossary_term: Operator ETOPS (Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards) Experience Level
description: The operator's ETOPS experience classification at the time of this authorization. Initial operators may have more restrictive conditions; advanced operators may have fewer limitations.
Additional free-text remarks, notes, or clarifications related to this ETOPS authorization. May include historical context, amendment notes, or operational guidance.

Free-text field capturing any special conditions, operational limitations, or additional requirements imposed by the regulatory authority on this ETOPS authorization (e.g., seasonal restrictions, crew qualification requirements, alternate airport specifications).
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA AC 120-42B, EASA Part-CAT

description: For certain ETOPS authorizations, the approved extension time (in minutes) for time-limited systems (e.g., cargo fire suppression discharge time). Nullable if not applicable.
reference: ICAO Annex 6, FAA AC 120-42B, EASA Part-CAT

description: Unique identifier for each daily aircraft utilization record. Primary key for the aircraft utilization ledger.
Foreign key reference to the aircraft master data. Links this utilization record to the specific aircraft asset.

Foreign key linking to finance.cost_centre. Business justification: Daily aircraft utilization drives cost allocation (fuel, crew, maintenance reserves). Finance allocates costs by aircraft to cost centres for profitability analysis, CASK calculation, and route/fleet

Foreign key linking to airport.station. Business justification: Daily utilization records require station context for station-level performance reporting, cost center allocation, and operational efficiency analysis. Critical for hub vs spoke utilization comparison
Total APU operating hours on this date. APU provides ground power and air conditioning when main engines are off. Tracked for APU maintenance intervals.
Rolling 30-day average daily block hours for this aircraft, calculated as of this utilization date. Used for fleet planning and lease utilization benchmarking.
Total block hours flown on this date, measured from gate departure (off-blocks) to gate arrival (on-blocks). Primary utilization metric for lease agreements and maintenance planning.

Indicates the quality and completeness of this utilization record. Verified indicates data has been validated against multiple sources, estimated indicates calculated or interpolated values, incomplete indicates missing data elements, reconciled indicates discrepancies have been resolved.

The source system from which this utilization data was captured. ACARS (Aircraft Communications Addressing and Reporting System) provides automated flight data, FMS (Flight Management System) provides flight planning data, AMOS (Aircraft Maintenance and Engineering System) provides maintenance records, manual indicates operator-entered data.
Total delay minutes attributed to this aircraft on this date, summed across all flights. Used for OTP (On-Time Performance) analysis and aircraft reliability tracking.

business_glossary_term: Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards (ETOPS) Sectors
description: Number of ETOPS-certified flight sectors operated on this date. ETOPS allows twin-engine aircraft to fly routes more than 60 minutes from the nearest suitable airport.
reference: ICAO Annex 6 Part I, FAA AC 120-42B - ETOPS

description: Total number of flight cycles (pressurization cycles) completed on this date. One cycle equals one takeoff and landing. Critical for fatigue-sensitive component maintenance.
reference: FAA AC 120-16F - Air Carrier Maintenance Programs

description: Total airborne flight hours on this date, measured from wheels-off to wheels-on. Used for airframe and engine maintenance interval calculations.
reference: FAA AC 120-16F - Air Carrier Maintenance Programs

description: Total fuel consumed (in kilograms) on this date across all flights. Used for CASK calculation and environmental reporting.
Total ground time (turnaround time) between flights on this date, summed across all sectors. Used for ground operations efficiency analysis.
Total number of landings performed on this date. Used for landing gear and brake maintenance tracking.
reference: FAA AC 120-16F - Air Carrier Maintenance Programs

description: Number of open MEL items (deferred defects) at the end of this utilization date. MEL allows aircraft to operate with certain non-critical systems inoperative under specific conditions.
reference: FAA AC 120-16F - Air Carrier Maintenance Programs, MMEL/MEL

description: Total flight hours operated during night hours (as defined by local aviation authority) on this date. Used for crew scheduling and fatigue management compliance.
reference: ICAO Annex 6 Part I - Operation of Aircraft

description: Block hours flown on non-revenue flights including ferry, positioning, maintenance test flights, and crew training.

The operational status of the aircraft on this date. In_service indicates normal revenue operations, maintenance indicates scheduled or unscheduled maintenance, AOG (Aircraft on Ground) indicates unplanned downtime, storage indicates temporary or long-term parking, ferry indicates non-revenue positioning.
Number of pilot-reported maintenance discrepancies logged on this date. Used for aircraft reliability trending and predictive maintenance.
reference: FAA AC 120-16F - Air Carrier Maintenance Programs

description: Timestamp when this utilization record was first created in the system. Used for data lineage and audit trail.
Timestamp when this utilization record was last modified. Used for change tracking and data quality monitoring.
Block hours flown on revenue-generating flights (passenger or cargo). Excludes ferry, positioning, and maintenance flights.
Total number of flight sectors (legs) operated on this date. Equivalent to number of departures.
The unique aircraft registration identifier (tail number) for operational reference. Denormalized from aircraft master for query performance.
reference: ICAO Annex 7 - Aircraft Nationality and Registration Marks

description: Delay minutes caused by technical or maintenance issues on this date. Subset of total delay minutes, used for aircraft reliability and maintenance effectiveness analysis.

business_glossary_term: Total Airframe Hours Time Since New (TSN)
description: Cumulative total airframe hours since the aircraft was manufactured, as of the end of this utilization date. Running total used for major maintenance checks and lease return conditions.
reference: FAA AC 120-16F - Air Carrier Maintenance Programs

description: Cumulative total flight cycles since the aircraft was manufactured, as of the end of this utilization date. Running total used for fatigue-sensitive component tracking.
reference: FAA AC 120-16F - Air Carrier Maintenance Programs

description: The calendar date (UTC) for which this utilization record applies. Each aircraft has one record per operational day.
Unique identifier for the cabin configuration record. Primary key.
Reference to the specific aircraft tail number to which this cabin configuration is installed.
Reference to the aircraft type (e.g., B738, A320) for which this configuration is designed. Used when configuration applies to a fleet type rather than a single tail.
Number of seats in the Business Class cabin. Zero if Business Class is not offered in this configuration.
Average seat pitch in Business Class, measured in inches. Null if Business Class is not offered.
Average seat width in Business Class, measured in inches. Null if Business Class is not offered.
Number of distinct cabin classes in this configuration (e.g., 1 for all-economy, 2 for business and economy, 3 for first/business/economy).
Unique business identifier for the cabin configuration version (e.g., B738-CFG-A, A320-Y180). Used for operational reference and inventory management.
Human-readable name or description of the cabin configuration (e.g., 'High-Density Economy', 'Premium Three-Class').

Current lifecycle status of the cabin configuration. Active configurations are in operational use; planned configurations are approved but not yet installed; retired configurations are no longer in service.
Timestamp when this cabin configuration record was first created in the system.
Total number of passenger entry/exit doors in this cabin configuration. Critical for emergency evacuation certification.
Number of seats in the Economy (standard coach) cabin.
Average seat pitch in Economy class, measured in inches.
Average seat width in Economy class, measured in inches.
Date on which this cabin configuration became (or will become) active and available for operational use.
Number of seats in the First Class cabin. Zero if First Class is not offered in this configuration.
Average seat pitch (distance from one seat to the same point on the seat in front) in First Class, measured in inches. Null if First Class is not offered.
Average seat width in First Class, measured in inches. Null if First Class is not offered.
Total number of galleys (food and beverage preparation areas) installed in this cabin configuration.
Type or model of the In-Flight Entertainment system installed (e.g., 'Panasonic eX3', 'Thales AVANT', 'Streaming-only'). Null if no IFE is installed.
Total number of lavatories (restrooms) installed in this cabin configuration.
Indicates whether this configuration includes lie-flat seats (typically in First or Business Class). True if any cabin class offers lie-flat capability.
Timestamp when this cabin configuration record was last modified.
Number of overwing emergency exits in this cabin configuration. Used for evacuation planning and seating restrictions.

Indicates the availability of power outlets (AC or USB) for passenger devices. Values: none (no power outlets), selected_seats (available in premium cabins or select rows), all_seats (available at every seat).
Number of seats in the Premium Economy cabin. Zero if Premium Economy is not offered in this configuration.
Average seat pitch in Premium Economy, measured in inches. Null if Premium Economy is not offered.
Average seat width in Premium Economy, measured in inches. Null if Premium Economy is not offered.
Date on which this cabin configuration was retired from service. Null if the configuration is still active or planned.
Reference identifier or URL to the detailed seat map diagram for this configuration. Used by reservation systems and passenger-facing applications.
Total number of passenger seats installed in this cabin configuration across all classes. Critical for revenue management and Available Seat Kilometer (ASK) calculations.
Indicates whether in-flight Wi-Fi connectivity is available in this cabin configuration.
Name of the Wi-Fi service provider (e.g., 'Gogo', 'Viasat', 'Panasonic Avionics'). Null if Wi-Fi is not available.
Unique identifier for the aircraft engine asset. Primary key for the engine master record.
Reference to the aircraft on which this engine is currently installed. Null if engine is in spare pool or shop.

Foreign key linking to finance.cost_centre. Business justification: Engine MRO costs, PBH (power-by-hour) contract charges, and depreciation must be attributed to a cost centre for airline management accounting and CASK reporting by fleet type. Aviation finance contro

Foreign key linking to airport.station. Business justification: Off-wing engine inventory management and shop visit logistics require station tracking. Spare engine positioning, MRO coordination, and rotable pool management depend on knowing current engine locatio

Foreign key linking to finance.fixed_asset. Business justification: Engines are individually capitalized assets under IFRS/GAAP requiring their own fixed_asset records for depreciation, impairment testing, and disposal accounting. Engine acquisition_cost and current_b

Foreign key linking to finance.lease_contract. Business justification: Engines are often leased separately from airframes (power-by-hour contracts, standalone engine leases). Finance must track engine lease liabilities independently for IFRS16 compliance and cash flow fo
Original acquisition cost of this engine in the airline's reporting currency. Used for asset valuation, depreciation calculation, and financial reporting. Null if cost information is not tracked.
Date when this engine was acquired by the airline, either through purchase, lease commencement, or delivery from manufacturer. Used for asset accounting and depreciation.
Timestamp when this engine master record was first created in the system. Used for data lineage and audit trail purposes.
Flight cycles accumulated since the engine's last major shop visit or overhaul. Resets to zero after each shop visit. Used alongside TSLSV for shop visit planning.
reference: IATA Engine Maintenance Cost Working Group Standards

description: Total number of flight cycles (takeoff and landing pairs) accumulated by this engine since manufacture. One cycle equals one takeoff-landing sequence. Used for fatigue life tracking.
reference: FAA Advisory Circular AC 120-16 - Continuing Analysis and Surveillance

description: Current depreciated book value of this engine on the airline's balance sheet. Updated based on depreciation schedule and any impairment charges. Null if not tracked.

Current operational status of the engine. Installed (on-wing), spare (serviceable spare pool), QEC (Quick Engine Change kit), shop (undergoing MRO), beyond-repair (uneconomical to repair), retired (end of service life), or AOG (Aircraft on Ground - engine causing aircraft grounding). [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: installed|spare|QEC|shop|beyond-repair|retired|AOG — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
reference: IATA Maintenance Cost Task Force Standards

description: Manufacturer-assigned unique serial number for this engine. The globally unique identifier used for tracking, MRO, and lease return. Also known as ESN in aviation industry.

business_glossary_term: Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards (ETOPS) Eligible
description: Indicates whether this engine meets ETOPS certification requirements for extended over-water or remote area operations. True if engine is ETOPS-certified and current on all ETOPS maintenance requirements.

business_glossary_term: Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards (ETOPS) Rating Minutes
description: ETOPS diversion time rating in minutes (e.g., 120, 180, 207, 330). Defines maximum allowable distance from suitable alternate airport. Null if engine is not ETOPS-certified.
Date when this engine was installed on the current aircraft. Null if engine is not currently installed on an aircraft.
Date when this engine completed its most recent major shop visit or overhaul. Used to calculate TSLSV and CSLSV intervals.

Type of maintenance work performed during the last shop visit. Performance restoration (PR), life-limited parts replacement, hot section inspection (HSI), or full overhaul. Determines next shop visit scope.
reference: IATA Engine Maintenance Cost Working Group Standards

description: Date when the current engine lease agreement expires. Critical for return condition planning and lease extension negotiations. Null if engine is owned.
reference: IATA Airline Disclosure Guide - Leased Assets

description: Contractual maintenance condition requirements that must be met when returning this leased engine to the lessor. Typically specifies minimum remaining hours/cycles on LLPs and maintenance status. Null if owned.
reference: IATA Airline Disclosure Guide - Leased Assets

description: Remaining cycles until mandatory retirement of the most limiting life-limited part in this engine. Critical for shop visit planning and residual value assessment.
reference: FAA Advisory Circular AC 39-8 - Continued Airworthiness Assessments

description: Summary of remaining cycles for critical life-limited parts (LLP) in this engine. LLPs are components with mandatory retirement life limits due to fatigue considerations. Format typically includes part numbers and remaining cycles.
reference: FAA Advisory Circular AC 39-8 - Continued Airworthiness Assessments

description: Name of the engine manufacturer. Major manufacturers include CFM International, GE Aviation, Rolls-Royce, Pratt & Whitney, IAE (International Aero Engines), and Engine Alliance.
Indicates whether this engine has any active MEL items that allow aircraft dispatch with certain engine components inoperative under controlled conditions. True if MEL items are currently active.
reference: FAA Advisory Circular AC 120-91 - Airport Obstacle Analysis

description: Number of active MEL items currently logged against this engine. Zero indicates no MEL items. Higher counts may indicate declining engine health or deferred maintenance.
reference: FAA Advisory Circular AC 120-91 - Airport Obstacle Analysis

description: Specific engine model and variant designation (e.g., CFM56-7B27, LEAP-1A26, Trent 1000, PW4000). Defines thrust rating, fuel efficiency, and maintenance program applicability.

Name of the MRO facility or provider currently responsible for this engine's maintenance, or the facility where the engine is currently undergoing shop work. May be OEM, third-party MRO, or airline in-house facility.
Planned or forecast date when this engine is scheduled for its next major shop visit or overhaul. Based on maintenance program intervals and current utilization rates.

Legal ownership and financial arrangement for this engine. Owned (airline owns outright), finance_lease (capital lease with ownership transfer), operating_lease (rental), power_by_hour (pay-per-use MRO contract), or spare_lease (short-term spare engine rental).
reference: IATA Airline Disclosure Guide - Leased Assets

description: Current position of the engine. For installed engines: 1, 2, 3, or 4 (left to right facing forward). For uninstalled: spare (serviceable spare pool), shop (undergoing MRO), or QEC (Quick Engine Change kit assembly). [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: 1|2|3|4|spare|shop|QEC — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Indicates whether this engine is covered under a power-by-hour (PBH) or similar pay-per-use maintenance contract with the OEM or MRO provider. True if under PBH contract.
reference: IATA Engine Maintenance Cost Working Group Standards

description: Free-text field for additional notes, special conditions, or operational remarks about this engine. May include historical events, special handling requirements, or configuration notes.
Date when this engine was last removed from an aircraft. Used to track off-wing time and shop visit intervals.
Maximum certified thrust output of this engine variant measured in kilonewtons (kN). International standard unit for thrust measurement.
Maximum certified thrust output of this engine variant measured in pounds-force (lbf). Determines aircraft performance capabilities and operational limitations.

business_glossary_term: Time Since Last Shop Visit (TSLSV) Hours
description: Flight hours accumulated since the engine's last major shop visit or overhaul. Resets to zero after each shop visit. Used to plan next shop visit interval.
reference: IATA Engine Maintenance Cost Working Group Standards

description: Total flight hours accumulated by this engine since it was manufactured (Time Since New). Critical metric for life-limited parts tracking and residual value calculation.
reference: FAA Advisory Circular AC 120-16 - Continuing Analysis and Surveillance

description: Timestamp when this engine master record was last modified. Updated whenever any attribute of the engine record changes. Used for change tracking and data synchronization.
Date when the manufacturer's warranty coverage for this engine expires. After this date, all maintenance costs are borne by the airline or covered under separate maintenance agreements.
Unique identifier for the seat map record. Primary key for the seat map entity.
Reference to the specific aircraft configuration that this seat belongs to. Links to the aircraft configuration master data defining the overall cabin layout.
The reason why the seat is blocked from assignment. Provides operational context for seat inventory management and passenger service recovery.
The service class or cabin compartment to which this seat is assigned. Determines service level, amenities, and fare eligibility.
The alphabetic column identifier for the seat position within the row (A through K). Typically A and K are window seats in wide-body aircraft.
The timestamp when this seat map record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail and data lineage.
The deck or level of the aircraft where the seat is located. Relevant for wide-body aircraft with multiple passenger decks.
The date from which this seat map configuration becomes active and available for passenger assignment. Supports configuration versioning and fleet changes.
The date on which this seat map configuration is retired and no longer valid for passenger assignment. Nullable for current active configurations.
Indicates the proximity of the seat to galley (kitchen) facilities. Proximity to galleys can impact passenger experience due to noise and crew activity.
Flag indicating whether the seat is equipped with an in-flight entertainment system. IFE availability is a key amenity for passenger satisfaction and ancillary revenue.
The diagonal screen size in inches of the in-flight entertainment display at this seat. Used for product differentiation and passenger communication.
Flag indicating whether the seat is designated as accessible for passengers with disabilities or reduced mobility. Accessible seats meet regulatory requirements for space and proximity to facilities.
Flag indicating whether the seat position can accommodate an infant bassinet attachment. Typically available at bulkhead seats with sufficient wall space.
Flag indicating whether the seat is currently blocked from passenger assignment. Blocked seats are unavailable for sale or assignment due to operational, regulatory, or commercial reasons.
Flag indicating whether the seat is located directly behind a bulkhead (dividing wall). Bulkhead seats typically offer extra legroom but may have restricted under-seat storage.
Flag indicating whether the seat is designated for crew rest on long-haul flights. Crew rest seats are blocked from passenger assignment and reserved for flight crew during rest periods.
Flag indicating whether the seat is located in an emergency exit row. Exit row seats have special regulatory requirements and passenger eligibility restrictions.
Flag indicating whether the seat is in the last row of the cabin or section. Last row seats often have limited or no recline and proximity to lavatories.
Flag indicating whether the seat is located over the aircraft wing, which may restrict window views. Used for passenger communication and seat selection preferences.
Flag indicating whether the seat is designated as a preferred or premium seat eligible for ancillary seat selection fees. Preferred seats typically offer enhanced comfort or location.
The timestamp when this seat map record was last updated. Used for change tracking and audit trail.
Indicates the proximity of the seat to lavatory facilities. Proximity to lavatories can be a positive (convenience) or negative (noise, traffic) factor for seat selection.
The type of power outlet available at the seat for passenger device charging. Power availability is increasingly important for passenger satisfaction and ancillary revenue.
The maximum recline angle of the seat in degrees from vertical. Used to communicate seat comfort specifications and differentiate premium products.
The recline capability of the seat, ranging from no recline to full lie-flat configurations. Critical for premium cabin differentiation and passenger comfort.
The numeric row identifier within the aircraft cabin. Used for seat assignment logic and cabin zone management.
The unique seat identifier within the aircraft, combining row number and column letter (e.g., 12A, 34F). This is the seat label passengers see on their boarding pass.
The pricing tier for ancillary seat selection fees. Used by revenue management systems to price seat assignments and optimize ancillary revenue.
The current lifecycle status of the seat map record. Indicates whether the seat configuration is currently in use, pending activation, or retired.
The distance in inches from one point on a seat to the same point on the seat in front or behind, measuring legroom and seat spacing. Key metric for passenger comfort and cabin density.

The positional type of the seat within the row, indicating whether it is adjacent to the window, in the middle of a seat group, or adjacent to the aisle. Critical for passenger preference and ancillary revenue.
The width of the seat in inches, measured at the widest point of the seat cushion. Impacts passenger comfort and cabin configuration.
Indicates any restrictions on carry-on baggage storage at this seat location. Some seats (e.g., bulkhead, exit row) have limited under-seat or overhead storage.
Unique identifier for the aircraft delivery event. Primary key for the aircraft delivery transaction.
Reference to the aircraft master record that was delivered. Links to the aircraft asset registry.

Foreign key linking to fleet.aircraft_lease. Business justification: An aircraft delivery event — particularly for wet-lease, dry-lease, or ACMI arrangements — fulfills a specific lease agreement. Linking aircraft_delivery.aircraft_lease_id → aircraft_lease.aircraft_le

Foreign key linking to fleet.aircraft_type. Business justification: Aircraft delivery records the type of aircraft being delivered. Currently denormalizes aircraft_type_icao and aircraft_type_iata. Adding aircraft_type_id FK normalizes this and allows delivery records

Foreign key linking to fleet.cabin_configuration. Business justification: aircraft_delivery currently stores cabin_configuration_code as a plain STRING, denormalizing the cabin layout installed at delivery. The cabin_configuration table is the authoritative source for confi

Foreign key linking to airport.station. Business justification: New aircraft acceptance logistics, delivery team deployment, and acceptance flight planning require station linkage. Fleet planning and delivery coordination depend on accurate station tracking. Repla

Foreign key linking to finance.fixed_asset. Business justification: When aircraft is delivered, it's capitalized as a fixed asset. Delivery record must link to fixed_asset entry for acquisition cost, depreciation tracking, and asset register reconciliation. Real busin

Foreign key linking to fleet.fleet_order. Business justification: Aircraft delivery fulfills a fleet order. fleet_order tracks purchase orders and lease commitments; aircraft_delivery records the actual delivery event. Linking delivery to order establishes traceabil
Reference number of the formal acceptance certificate document signed by the airline and manufacturer/lessor confirming aircraft delivery and acceptance.
Total accumulated flight cycles (takeoff and landing cycles) on the airframe at the time of delivery. Zero for new aircraft, non-zero for used aircraft.
Total accumulated flight hours (block hours) on the airframe at the time of delivery. Zero for new aircraft, non-zero for used aircraft.
Manufacturer serial number of the Auxiliary Power Unit (APU) installed on the aircraft at delivery. APU provides electrical power and pneumatic air on the ground and in-flight backup.
Timestamp when this aircraft delivery record was first created in the system.
Condition classification of the aircraft at delivery: new from manufacturer, used from lessor, refurbished after major overhaul, or pre-owned from another operator.
The date on which the aircraft was formally accepted into the airline's fleet from the manufacturer or lessor. Marks the start of operational availability.
Flight number assigned to the ferry flight that delivered the aircraft from the manufacturer or lessor facility to the airline's base. May be a non-revenue positioning flight.

Free-text notes capturing additional details about the delivery event, including any special conditions, snags identified during acceptance inspection, or deviations from standard delivery procedures.
Externally-known unique business identifier for this aircraft delivery transaction, typically assigned by the manufacturer or lessor.

Current lifecycle status of the aircraft delivery transaction: scheduled (planned future delivery), in transit (ferry flight underway), accepted (delivery completed and aircraft in service), rejected (delivery refused), cancelled (delivery cancelled), or deferred (delivery postponed).
Precise timestamp when the aircraft delivery acceptance was signed off and the aircraft officially transferred to the airline's operational control.
Manufacturer serial number of the engine installed in position 1 (typically left engine on twin-engine aircraft) at delivery.
Manufacturer serial number of the engine installed in position 2 (typically right engine on twin-engine aircraft) at delivery.
Manufacturer serial number of the engine installed in position 3 (for tri-jet or quad-jet aircraft) at delivery. Null for twin-engine aircraft.
Manufacturer serial number of the engine installed in position 4 (for quad-jet aircraft) at delivery. Null for twin-engine and tri-jet aircraft.

business_glossary_term: Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards (ETOPS) Certification Flag
description: Indicates whether the aircraft is certified for ETOPS operations at delivery, allowing extended-range twin-engine flights over routes distant from diversion airports.
reference: FAA Part 121 Appendix P, EASA Part-CAT

business_glossary_term: Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards (ETOPS) Rating in Minutes
description: ETOPS rating in minutes (e.g., 120, 180, 207, 330) indicating the maximum diversion time the aircraft is certified for. Null if not ETOPS certified.
reference: FAA Part 121 Appendix P, EASA Part-CAT

description: Indicates whether a Minimum Equipment List (MEL) is applicable to this aircraft, allowing dispatch with certain non-critical equipment inoperative under specified conditions.
Unique serial number assigned by the aircraft manufacturer at production. Permanent identifier for the airframe throughout its lifecycle.

Type of ownership or lease arrangement under which the aircraft was acquired: owned (purchased outright), wet lease (aircraft with crew), dry lease (aircraft only), ACMI (Aircraft Crew Maintenance Insurance), finance lease, or operating lease.
Civil aviation registration number (tail number) assigned to the aircraft at delivery. Displayed on the aircraft fuselage and used for ATC identification.

Outcome of the technical acceptance inspection performed by the airline's engineering team: accepted (no issues), accepted with conditions (minor snags to be resolved), rejected (major defects), or deferred (inspection postponed).
Total number of passenger seats installed in the aircraft at delivery, across all cabin classes.
Timestamp when this aircraft delivery record was last modified in the system.
Unique identifier for the aircraft lease agreement record. Primary key for the aircraft lease entity.
Reference to the specific aircraft asset covered by this lease agreement. Links to the aircraft master registry.

Foreign key linking to fleet.aircraft_type. Business justification: aircraft_lease currently stores aircraft_type_code as a plain STRING, which is a denormalized reference to the aircraft type. Lease agreements are often structured around a specific aircraft type (e.g

Foreign key linking to fleet.fleet_order. Business justification: A lease agreement often originates from a fleet order commitment (when fleet_order.order_type = lease commitment). Linking aircraft_lease.fleet_order_id → fleet_order.fleet_order_id allows the lease r

Foreign key linking to finance.lease_contract. Business justification: aircraft_lease is the operational lease record; lease_contract is the financial/accounting representation under IFRS16. Same lease must exist in both domains for compliance. Real business process: lea
Reference to the lessee entity (airline operating unit or subsidiary) receiving the aircraft. Links to internal organizational hierarchy.

Foreign key linking to route.block_time_standard. Business justification: Flight dispatchers reference the authoritative block_time_standard when computing fuel requirements, crew duty time limits, and planned block hours in a flight plan. This is a direct regulatory and op

Foreign key linking to flight.flight_leg. Business justification: Flight plans are operationally tied to specific flight legs (actual operations on a date), not just the schedule master. A flight plan is prepared for a specific operational instance. This FK links th
Reference to the pilot-in-command who accepted and signed off on the operational flight plan.
Reference to the specific flight leg for which this flight plan was generated.
Four-letter ICAO code of the primary alternate airport in case destination is unavailable.
Four-letter ICAO code of the secondary alternate airport.
Fuel in kilograms required to fly from destination to the alternate airport and conduct an approach and landing.
Four-letter ICAO code of the destination airport.
Contingency fuel in kilograms to account for unforeseen factors (typically 5% of trip fuel or 5 minutes holding fuel, whichever is greater).
Cost index value used for flight planning optimization, balancing time cost versus fuel cost. Higher values favor speed; lower values favor fuel efficiency.
reference: Boeing Flight Planning and Performance Manual

description: Date and time when this flight plan record was first created in the system.
Planned cruise altitude in feet above mean sea level (MSL) for the primary cruise segment.
reference: ICAO Annex 2 - Rules of the Air

description: Planned cruise speed in knots (true airspeed or indicated airspeed as per flight planning convention).
Four-letter ICAO code of the departure airport.

business_glossary_term: Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards (ETOPS) Entry Point
description: Waypoint or geographic coordinate where the flight enters ETOPS airspace.

business_glossary_term: Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards (ETOPS) Exit Point
description: Waypoint or geographic coordinate where the flight exits ETOPS airspace.

business_glossary_term: Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards (ETOPS) Qualified Flag
description: Indicates whether this flight plan is qualified for ETOPS operations (extended-range twin-engine flights over water or remote areas).
Date and time when this flight plan version was filed with Air Traffic Control (ATC) or ICAO Flight Plan Processing System.
reference: ICAO Annex 2 - Rules of the Air

description: Final reserve fuel in kilograms: minimum fuel required to fly for 30 minutes at holding speed at 1,500 feet above alternate airport elevation in standard conditions.
Planned landing weight in kilograms at destination: takeoff weight minus trip fuel burn.
Date and time when this flight plan record was last updated.
Date and time when this flight plan version was accepted by the crew and loaded into the Flight Management System (FMS) as the operational flight plan (OFP).

Current lifecycle status of the flight plan. Filed: submitted to ATC/ICAO; Amended: re-cleared or updated; Operational: final crew-accepted OFP loaded into FMS for execution; Cancelled: plan withdrawn; Rejected: not accepted by ATC; Superseded: replaced by newer version.
Estimated total block time in hours from gate departure (Out) to gate arrival (In), including taxi, flight, and approach time.
Estimated airborne time in hours from wheels-off to wheels-on.
Estimated taxi time in minutes from landing to gate arrival.
Estimated taxi time in minutes from gate departure to takeoff.
Full textual representation of the planned route including RNAV waypoints, airways, Standard Instrument Departures (SIDs), and Standard Terminal Arrival Routes (STARs).
Planned altitude step-climb profile showing waypoints and altitudes where the aircraft will request higher cruise levels to optimize fuel efficiency as weight decreases.
Planned takeoff weight in kilograms: zero fuel weight plus total fuel on board at brake release.
Total fuel required in kilograms: sum of trip fuel, contingency fuel, alternate fuel, final reserve fuel, taxi fuel, and any additional fuel.
Planned fuel burn in kilograms from takeoff to landing at destination airport.
Sequential version number of this flight plan. Increments with each amendment or update to the plan for the same flight.
Source of wind and temperature forecast data used in flight planning (e.g., NOAA GFS, ECMWF, proprietary meteorological service).
reference: ICAO Annex 3 - Meteorological Service for International Air Navigation

description: Planned zero fuel weight in kilograms: aircraft operating empty weight plus payload (passengers, cargo, baggage) but excluding all usable fuel.
Unique identifier for the scheduled flight record. Primary key for the scheduled flight master data.

Foreign key linking to fleet.aircraft_type. Business justification: Schedule planning systems require aircraft type master data (seating capacity, range, performance) to validate route feasibility and publish accurate capacity. aircraft_type_code is denormalized looku

Foreign key linking to fleet.cabin_configuration. Business justification: Airlines publish seat inventory and booking classes against a specific cabin configuration per scheduled flight. Revenue management, GDS distribution, and seat map display all require resolving the ex

Foreign key linking to route.fleet_assignment. Business justification: Schedule build and fleet planning require tracing each scheduled_flight back to the fleet_assignment decision that drove it. Network planners and revenue management teams reference fleet assignments f

Foreign key linking to route.flight_number. Business justification: GDS distribution, codeshare setup, and revenue management class assignment all require linking a scheduled_flight to its authoritative flight_number record. flight_leg already has this FK; scheduled_f

Foreign key linking to route.carrier. Business justification: Codeshare operations, GDS distribution, and interline settlement require identifying the marketing carrier on a scheduled_flight. Role prefix 'marketing_' distinguishes from operating carrier. marketi

Foreign key linking to route.route. Business justification: Scheduled flights operate on defined routes. This FK links the flight schedule to the route master for schedule planning, slot coordination, and network analysis.

Foreign key linking to route.schedule_season. Business justification: Schedule planning, IATA slot coordination, and OAG filing all require associating each scheduled_flight with its IATA schedule season. Aviation planners and slot coordinators always work within a seas

Foreign key linking to airport.slot. Business justification: IATA slot coordination requires each scheduled flight service to be assigned a specific airport slot. Schedule planning teams link scheduled flights to allocated slots for season filing, historic prec
Three-letter IATA airport code for the scheduled arrival airport (e.g., LAX, CDG, SIN).
Scheduled arrival terminal identifier at the destination airport (e.g., Terminal 2, T5, Concourse A).
Planned block time in minutes from gate departure (Out) to gate arrival (In). This is the scheduled elapsed time for the flight leg.
Flag indicating whether this scheduled flight is part of a codeshare agreement where multiple airlines market the same flight operation. True if codeshare, false if single-carrier operation.
Timestamp when this scheduled flight record was first created in the system. Audit field for data lineage and compliance.
Seven-character string indicating which days of the week the flight operates (1=operates, 0=does not operate). Position 1=Monday through position 7=Sunday (e.g., 1111100 = Monday-Friday only).
reference: IATA SSIM Day of Week Encoding

description: Three-letter IATA airport code for the scheduled departure airport (e.g., JFK, LHR, DXB).
Scheduled departure terminal identifier at the origin airport (e.g., Terminal 1, T3, Concourse B).
The first date this scheduled flight is valid and may operate. Start of the schedule validity period.
The last date this scheduled flight is valid and may operate. End of the schedule validity period. Nullable for open-ended schedules.

business_glossary_term: Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards (ETOPS) Approval Indicator
description: Flag indicating whether this scheduled flight route requires ETOPS certification for twin-engine aircraft operations over extended distances from diversion airports. True if ETOPS-certified route required.
Great circle distance between departure and arrival airports in kilometers. Used for RPK and ASK calculations.
Timestamp when this scheduled flight record was last updated. Audit field for change tracking and data quality monitoring.
Single-character IATA code indicating the type of meal service provided: B=Breakfast, L=Lunch, D=Dinner, S=Snack, M=Meal (unspecified), N=No meal service.

Minimum time in minutes required for passengers to make a legal connection from this flight to another flight at the arrival airport. Varies by airport, terminal, and connection type (domestic-domestic, domestic-international, international-international).
Two-character IATA airline code of the carrier actually operating the flight (e.g., AA for American Airlines, BA for British Airways).
Date when this scheduled flight was first published to Global Distribution Systems (GDS) and made available for booking. Critical for revenue management and distribution.

Status of regulatory approval from civil aviation authorities for this scheduled flight route and slot allocation: approved (fully authorized), pending (awaiting approval), conditional (approved with restrictions), or denied (not authorized).

Classification of the scheduled flight within the airlines route network strategy: hub_spoke (connecting through a hub), point_to_point (direct city-pair), regional_feeder (short-haul feed to hub), long_haul_international (intercontinental), or domestic_trunk (major domestic route).

Current lifecycle status of the scheduled flight: active (currently operating), suspended (temporarily not operating), cancelled (permanently removed), seasonal_inactive (out of season), or pending_approval (awaiting regulatory approval).
Version number of this schedule record. Incremented each time the scheduled flight details are modified (time changes, equipment changes, etc.). Supports schedule change tracking and audit.
Published scheduled arrival time in local airport time. This is the planned gate arrival time (In time in OOOI).
Published scheduled departure time in local airport time. This is the planned gate departure time (Out time in OOOI).

Classification of the flight service: passenger (revenue passenger service), cargo (freight-only), ferry (non-revenue repositioning), charter (non-scheduled charter), or positioning (crew/aircraft repositioning).
IATA traffic restriction code indicating any limitations on passenger or cargo carriage (e.g., C=Cargo only, G=Technical stop no traffic rights, F=Passenger service only). Empty if no restrictions.
Flag indicating whether this scheduled flight is operated under a wet lease agreement (aircraft leased with crew, maintenance, and insurance from another carrier). True if wet lease operation.
Unique identifier for the weight and balance loadsheet record. Primary key.

Foreign key linking to airport.station. Business justification: Loadsheets are prepared by the departure station's load control team. Linking weight_balance to the departure station enables station-level load control audit trails, regulatory compliance reporting,

Foreign key linking to flight.dispatch_release. Business justification: Weight & balance loadsheet is part of the dispatch release package in aviation operations. The W&B must be coordinated with and approved as part of the dispatch release. This is a standard operational
